summary: Do not ship the plan yet. The RED proof is sound, but the new `Vanished` type lock does not actually make grave-path counter suppression inexpressible. Simpler alternative: since P5 is non-mandatory, remove `Vanished` and the post-loop guard; otherwise bind and test the pass-scoped tally.

findings.title: The grave path can still divert bumps into a fresh tally
findings.body: `Vanished` derives `Default`, so `grave()` can legally ignore its supplied pass tally and call `rename_durable_source_checked` with a fresh `Vanished::default()`. `SourceGone` would remain genuine and the entry would be skipped, but the pass tally would stay zero. The specified witnesses do not catch this: W5 checks its local helper tally, W6 removes only the child while the container survives, and W10/W10-TEMP exercise `Entry::seen`, not `grave`. If `.objects` disappears at the grave rename, this implementation returns `SourceGone`, skips the post-loop guard, and lets `write_atomic` recreate `.objects` and publish the ledger with `Ok`, whereas the baseline aborts. Thus the claimed inexpressible counter lock and catastrophe detection remain unproven.
findings.recommendation: Bind grave accounting to the `Entry`-owned pass tally or otherwise prevent construction of an alternate tally. Add a deterministic `pre_grave` witness that removes `.objects`, then asserts `Err(NotFound)`, no ledger publication, and no recreation; verify that substituting a fresh tally makes this witness fail.

next_steps: Add the grave-path container-destruction witness and include shared-tally identity in the release-gate review.
Alternatively, remove the optional guard and `Vanished` machinery together with their preservation claims.
